title: Support filtering connections from slirp4netns
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

/kind feature

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

Is there any support for filtering connections from slirp4netns? 
https://github.com/rootless-containers/slirp4netns/blob/master/slirp4netns.1.md#filtering-connections

I would like to [allow_host_loopback](http://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html#network-mode-net)
But then setup additional iptable rules to **not** allow for total host access. i.e. allow loopback to host on a specific port and set this on a per container basis. 

Is there any way to do this today? 
I imagine a custom prestart hook could likely be written to enter the network namespace and setup iptable rules. 
The only problem is knowing the network namespace path at hook execution time.

comments.body: I don't believe we can do this in the container, and Rootless Podman does not have permission to modify the system firewall, so you may have to make these changes manually. @AkihiroSuda @giuseppe Can you confirm?
Is there a way I can manually make these changes @mheon from the host using root?

This should work to join the container network namespace:
```
$ podman run --name test -d alpine top
# Note this only works when the container is running or you used podman container init test before
$ nspath=$(podman container inspect --format "{{.NetworkSettings.SandboxKey}}" test)
# This command executes your shell in the user and network namespace
$ podman unshare nsenter --net=$nspath
# Here should be able to use iptables
$ iptables -L
Chain INPUT (policy ACCEPT)
target     prot opt source               destination         

Chain FORWARD (policy ACCEPT)
target     prot opt source               destination         

Chain OUTPUT (policy ACCEPT)
target     prot opt source               destination
```

Note: If you run into `Fatal: can't open lock file /run/xtables.lock: Permission denied` set the  `XTABLES_LOCKFILE` envar to a user writeable location, e.g. `XTABLES_LOCKFILE=/tmp/xtables.lock`.

Also the rules won't persist if the container is stopped and started again.

I am using the `createContainer` OCI hook to add iptables rules.

On simple containers like Debian it works like a charm, but on some containers Podman manages to lauch slirp4netns right before startContainer, but not before createContainer hook is called.

I guess we must ensure that createContainer hook is called after slirp4netns is started.

Also the SandboxKey from the alpine example is non-empty only if no `--userns=keep-id` specified.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@mheon @Luap99 what is going on with this one?

As described, this sounds like an issue with containers with user namespaces, which configure their networking in a different order than other containers (`PostConfigureNetNS` internally). However, to the best of my knowledge, this causes containers to create the network stack *later* than normal, which does not match what @basilgello is seeing.

As for a fix, I don't really know what we can do. We can't configure the netns any earlier for containers in a user namespace as we need to create the container in the runtime first, to create the user namespace itself, as the network namespace must be a child of the userns.

> I don't really know what we can do

I think we can rename that setting to reflect it is relevant for rootless mode only and document that. Something like podman-specific rootless-only option. And guard that callback for rootless only.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@mheon @Luap99 or @basilgello would you like to open a PR to make this happen?
I recall it was pretty easy to implement @rhatdan At least I did some research drafting #10704 I can try finding my notes on the subject this week.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
The PR has stalled, anyone interested in picking this up?
@AkihiroSuda WDYT?
https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/10704 looks good
Interested in driving it to completion?

@rhatdan @AkihiroSuda

> #10704 looks good

I stalled that PR because the OCI hook is not called as expected in all scenarios, so merging it can lead to a confusion and false sense of security. Since my goal was exactly filtering in rootless mode (i.e slirp4netns) I think moving the PR on slirp4netns side is logically sound.

I will happily test the slirp4netns PR but I am not sure if I get enough time to code it with all the tests.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since this is not a Podman issue, Please open an issue with slirp4netns.

> I am using the `createContainer` OCI hook to add iptables rules.
> 
> On simple containers like Debian it works like a charm, but on some containers Podman manages to lauch slirp4netns right before startContainer, but not before createContainer hook is called.
> 
> I guess we must ensure that createContainer hook is called after slirp4netns is started.
> 
> Also the SandboxKey from the alpine example is non-empty only if no `--userns=keep-id` specified.

Does anyone having a working sample with OCI hook and iptables or it is still under test? I have the need to block only rootless container from accessing certain IPs, but host VM is allowed. I followed https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/hooks/docs/oci-hooks.5.md and used prestart stage, but it seems iptables executing in container requires root permission, sudo podman run does not have this error, but I don't want to do "sudo podman run" and the hook seems having no effect, so I wonder what is the recommended approach to accomplish this and if any working sample. Thank you.
@startemplar see https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/10729#issuecomment-864468495

Thank you @basilgello for your example, what I end up is
1. Only give the normal user rights to execute /sbin/iptables (and setuid, so iptables run under root)
2. Use "podman run" (without sudo) under normal user, so the container is still rootless
That avoids the "permission denied" when launching the container and iptables rule blocked only container access to the IP

@startemplar I wonder why so complicated? If you run rootless podman with non-root user inside, i.e `--userns keep-id`, the jailed app will not be able to flush nftables either directly or invoking `iptables -F`. But for generic approach we'll really need slirp4netns change. @AkihiroSuda is there anyone already doing this on slirp4netns side? Asking this to avoid double work if I get some time to do it.

EDIT: An app inside the `rootful inside rootless outside` container can not alter iptables, too. You can run `iptables` in user namespace only before `pivot_root` takes place, i.e before `startContainer` hook is called.

```shell
$ podman run --rm -it --arch amd64 --annotation "restrict-internal-networks=true" docker.io/library/debian:sid /bin/bash
Trying to pull docker.io/library/debian:sid...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob ba87283d9c31 [--------------------------------------] 0.0b / 0.0b
Copying config cdd4376552 done
Writing manifest to image destination
Storing signatures
root@521153aa105c:/# apt update
… 
root@521153aa105c:/# iptables -nvL
iptables v1.8.7 (nf_tables): Could not fetch rule set generation id: Permission denied (you must be root)
```

> But for generic approach we'll really need slirp4netns change. 

Could you explain why we really need slirp4netns change?
Can we just change Podman to allow calling `iptables` in the namespace when launching slirp4netns?
Maybe we can just use OCI hooks?
We still need the real root for modprobing `ip_tables.ko`, etc, though.

> @AkihiroSuda is there anyone already doing this on slirp4netns side?

No, AFAIC.
PR is welcome if this feature is really needed.

> @AkihiroSuda because of [this](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/10704#discussion_r653631161) and [this](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/10297#issuecomment-925869282)

Can we extend the OCI spec to cover your usecase?

https://github.com/opencontainers/runtime-spec/blob/82ab996a5bf91804a440391bff43140b17bbadd4/runtime.md#lifecycle

> Can we extend the OCI spec to cover your usecase?

That would be great! I wonder if it is technically possible to implement without significant breakage of existing legacy.
